contexts.text: It's January — New Year's resolutions about being more social are already crumbling.
Summer in Ontario means patios, festivals, and the unique loneliness of watching everyone else seem to have plans.
The holidays amplify loneliness — this is peak season for people re-evaluating their social lives.
Spring in Ontario brings that restless energy where everyone suddenly wants to 'get out more,' but nobody knows where to start.
Fall is when the social calendar resets — summer plans evaporate, and the long indoor months start feeling inevitable.
Kitchener-Waterloo's tech scene has created a wave of young transplants who don't know anyone.
Toronto's density creates an illusion of social abundance that makes actual connection harder.
Ontario's long winters create seasonal isolation patterns that compound existing loneliness.
Remote work is now the default for many, and water-cooler friendships have disappeared with it.
The Surgeon General called loneliness an epidemic — so why does nobody talk about it casually?
Gen Z is the first generation to grow up with friendship mediated by algorithms.
Angle this toward someone in their late 20s who just realized their college friend group has quietly dissolved.
Write for someone who moved cities for a job and has been 'meaning to put themselves out there' for six months.
Target someone who's socially functional at work but has zero close friends outside of it.
